content:
# Genesis Chapter 43 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 25v–26r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_043_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 43–44 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0047 | https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0048

---

*The Famine Presses — Jacob Reluctant to Send Benjamin (vv. 1–5)*

**43:1** And the famine [וְהָרָעָב] was heavy [כָּבֵד] in the land [בָּאָרֶץ].

**43:2** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when they had finished [כַּאֲשֶׁר כִּלּוּ] eating [לֶאֱכֹל] the grain [אֶת-הַשֶּׁבֶר] that they had brought from Egypt [אֲשֶׁר הֵבִיאוּ מִמִּצְרָיִם] — that their father said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵיהֶם אֲבִיהֶם]: Return [שֻׁבוּ] — buy for us [שִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ] a little food [מְעַט-אֹכֶל].

**43:3** And Judah said to him [וַיֹּאמֶר אֵלָיו יְהוּדָה] saying [לֵאמֹר]: The man solemnly warned us [הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ הָאִישׁ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: You shall not see my face [לֹא-תִרְאוּ פָנַי] unless your brother is with you [בִּלְתִּי אֲחִיכֶם אִתְּכֶם].

**43:4** If you are sending [אִם-יֶשְׁךָ מְשַׁלֵּחַ] our brother with us [אֶת-אָחִינוּ אִתָּנוּ] — we will go down [נֵרֵד] and buy [וְנִשְׁבְּרָה-לְּךָ] food for you [אֹכֶל].

**43:5** But if you are not sending [וְאִם-אֵינְךָ מְשַׁלֵּחַ] — we will not go down [לֹא נֵרֵד] — for the man said to us [כִּי-הָאִישׁ אָמַר אֵלֵינוּ]: You shall not see my face [לֹא-תִרְאוּ פָנַי] unless your brother is with you [בִּלְתִּי אֲחִיכֶם אִתְּכֶם].

---

*Judah's Surety Speech — Jacob Relents (vv. 6–14)*

**43:6** And Israel said [וַיֹּאמֶר יִשְׂרָאֵל]: Why did you do evil to me [לָמָּה הֲרֵעֹתֶם לִי] by telling the man [לְהַגִּיד לָאִישׁ] that you had another brother [הַעוֹד לָכֶם אָח]?

**43:7** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: The man asked insistently [שָׁאוֹל שָׁאַל הָאִישׁ] about us and about our kindred [לָנוּ וּלְמוֹלַדְתֵּנוּ] saying [לֵאמֹר]: Is your father still alive [הַעוֹד אֲבִיכֶם חָי] — is there a brother to you [הֲיֵשׁ לָכֶם אָח] — and we told him [וַנַּגֶּד-לוֹ] according to the mouth of these words [עַל-פִּי הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה] — could we in any way know [הֲיָדוֹעַ נֵדַע] that he would say [כִּי יֹאמַר]: Bring your brother down [הוֹרִידוּ אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם]?

**43:8** And Judah said [וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוּדָה] to Israel his father [אֶל-יִשְׂרָאֵל אָבִיו]: Send the youth with me [שִׁלְחָה הַנַּעַר אִתִּי] and let us arise and go [וְנָקוּמָה וְנֵלֵכָה] — that we may live [וְנִחְיֶה] and not die [וְלֹא נָמוּת] — both we [גַּם-אֲנַחְנוּ] and also you [גַּם-אַתָּה] and also our little ones [גַּם-טַפֵּנוּ].

**43:9** I myself will be surety for him [אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ] — from my hand you may seek him [מִיָּדִי תְּבַקְשֶׁנּוּ] — if I do not bring him back to you [אִם-לֹא הֲבִיאֹתִיו אֵלֶיךָ] and set him before you [וְהִצַּגְתִּיו לְפָנֶיךָ] — then I will have sinned against you [וְחָטָאתִי לְךָ] all the days [כָּל-הַיָּמִים].

**43:10** For had we not delayed [כִּי לוּלֵי הִתְמַהְמַהְנוּ] — surely by now we would have returned [כִּי-עַתָּה שַׁבְנוּ] already twice [זֶה פַעֲמָיִם].

**43:11** And Israel their father said to them [וַיֹּאמֶר אֲלֵהֶם יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲבִיהֶם]: If it must be so [אִם-כֵּן אֵפוֹא] — then do this [זֹאת עֲשׂוּ] — take [קְחוּ] from the choicest produce of the land [מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ] in your vessels [בִּכְלֵיכֶם] and bring down [וְהוֹרִידוּ] to the man [לָאִישׁ] a gift [מִנְחָה] — a little balm [מְעַט צֳרִי] and a little honey [וּמְעַט דְּבַשׁ] — tragacanth [נְכֹאת] and laudanum [וָלֹט] — pistachio nuts [בָּטְנִים] and almonds [וּשְׁקֵדִים].

**43:12** And double silver [וְכֶסֶף מִשְׁנֶה] take in your hand [קְחוּ בְיֶדְכֶם] — and the silver that was returned [וְאֶת-הַכֶּסֶף הַמּוּשָׁב] in the mouth of your sacks [בְּפִי אַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם] — bring back in your hand [תָּשִׁיבוּ בְיֶדְכֶם] — perhaps it was an oversight [אוּלַי מִשְׁגֶּה הוּא].

**43:13** And your brother take [וְאֶת-אֲחִיכֶם קָחוּ] — and arise [וְקוּמוּ] — return to the man [שׁוּבוּ אֶל-הָאִישׁ].

**43:14** And El Shaddai [וְאֵל שַׁדַּי] — may he give you [יִתֵּן לָכֶם] mercy [רַחֲמִים] before the man [לִפְנֵי הָאִישׁ] — and he shall release to you [וְשִׁלַּח לָכֶם] your other brother [אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם אַחֵר] and Benjamin [וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין] — and I [וַאֲנִי] — as I am bereaved, I am bereaved [כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי].

---

*The Brothers Arrive in Egypt with Benjamin — Joseph Commands the Feast (vv. 15–17)*

**43:15** And the men took [וַיִּקְחוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] this gift [אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה הַזֹּאת] and double silver [וּמִשְׁנֶה-כֶסֶף] they took in their hand [לָקְחוּ בְיָדָם] and Benjamin [וְאֶת-בִּנְיָמִין] — and they arose [וַיָּקֻמוּ] and went down [וַיֵּרְדוּ] to Egypt [מִצְרַיִם] and stood [וַיַּעַמְדוּ] before Joseph [לִפְנֵי יוֹסֵף].

**43:16** And Joseph saw [וַיַּרְא יוֹסֵף] Benjamin with them [אִתָּם אֶת-בִּנְיָמִין] — and he said to him who was over his house [וַיֹּאמֶר לַאֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּיתוֹ]: Bring the men to the house [הָבֵא אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים הַבַּיְתָה] — and slaughter a slaughter [וּטְבֹחַ טֶבַח] and prepare [וְהָכֵן] — for with me [כִּי אִתִּי] these men shall eat [יֹאכְלוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] at noon [בַּצָּהֳרָיִם].

**43:17** And the man did [וַיַּעַשׂ הָאִישׁ] as Joseph said [כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמַר יוֹסֵף] and the man brought [וַיָּבֵא הָאִישׁ] the men [אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים] to Joseph's house [בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף].

---

*The Brothers Fear at Joseph's House — The Steward Reassures Them (vv. 18–23)*

**43:18** And the men feared [וַיִּירְאוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] because they were brought [כִּי הוּבְאוּ] to Joseph's house [בֵּית יוֹסֵף] and they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Because of the matter of the silver [עַל-דְּבַר הַכֶּסֶף] that was returned [הַמּוּשָׁב] in our sacks [בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ] at the first [בַּתְּחִלָּה] — we are brought in [אֲנַחְנוּ מוּבָאִים] — in order to roll against us [לְהִתְגֹּלֵל עָלֵינוּ] and to fall upon us [וּלְהִתְנַפֵּל עָלֵינוּ] and to take us as slaves [וְלָקַחַת אֹתָנוּ לַעֲבָדִים] and our donkeys [וְאֶת-חֲמֹרֵינוּ].

**43:19** And they drew near [וַיִּגְּשׁוּ] to the man who was over Joseph's house [אֶל-הָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר עַל-בֵּית יוֹסֵף] and they spoke to him [וַיְדַבְּרוּ אֵלָיו] at the entrance of the house [פֶּתַח הַבָּיִת].

**43:20** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Please [בִּי] my lord [אֲדֹנִי] — we came down indeed [יָרֹד יָרַדְנוּ] at the first [בַּתְּחִלָּה] to buy food [לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל].

**43:21** And it came to pass [וַיְהִי] when we came [כִּי-בָאנוּ] to the lodging place [אֶל-הַמָּלוֹן] and we opened [וַנִּפְתַּח] our sacks [אֶת-אַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ] — and behold [וְהִנֵּה] the silver of each man [כֶּסֶף-אִישׁ] was in the mouth of his sack [בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ] — our silver in its full weight [כַּסְפֵּנוּ בְּמִשְׁקָלוֹ] — and we have brought it back [וַנָּשֶׁב אֹתוֹ] in our hand [בְּיָדֵנוּ].

**43:22** And other silver [וְכֶסֶף אַחֵר] we have brought down [הוֹרַדְנוּ] in our hand [בְּיָדֵנוּ] to buy food [לִשְׁבָּר-אֹכֶל] — we do not know [לֹא יָדַעְנוּ] who placed [מִי-שָׂם] our silver [כַּסְפֵּנוּ] in our sacks [בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵינוּ].

**43:23** And he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Peace to you [שָׁלוֹם לָכֶם] — do not fear [אַל-תִּירָאוּ] — your God [אֱלֹהֵיכֶם] and the God of your father [וֵאלֹהֵי אֲבִיכֶם] has given you treasure [נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן] in your sacks [בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם] — your silver came to me [כַּסְפְּכֶם בָּא אֵלָי] — and he brought out [וַיּוֹצֵא] Simeon to them [אֲלֵיהֶם אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן].

---

*Reception at Joseph's House — The Gifts Presented (vv. 24–26)*

**43:24** And the man brought [וַיָּבֵא הָאִישׁ] the men [אֶת-הָאֲנָשִׁים] into Joseph's house [בֵּיתָה יוֹסֵף] and gave [וַיִּתֵּן] water [מַיִם] and they washed their feet [וַיִּרְחֲצוּ רַגְלֵיהֶם] — and he gave fodder [וַיִּתֵּן מִסְפּוֹא] to their donkeys [לַחֲמֹרֵיהֶם].

**43:25** And they prepared [וַיָּכִינוּ] the gift [אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה] until the coming of Joseph [עַד-בּוֹא יוֹסֵף] at noon [בַּצָּהֳרָיִם] — for they heard [כִּי שָׁמְעוּ] that they were to eat [כִּי-שָׁם יֹאכְלוּ] bread [לָחֶם].

**43:26** And Joseph came [וַיָּבֹא יוֹסֵף] to the house [הַבַּיְתָה] and they brought [וַיָּבִיאוּ] to him [לוֹ] the gift [אֶת-הַמִּנְחָה] that was in their hand [אֲשֶׁר-בְּיָדָם] — into the house [הַבַּיְתָה] — and they bowed down to him [וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ] to the ground [אָרְצָה].

---

*Joseph Inquires About His Father — He Sees Benjamin — He Weeps (vv. 27–31)*

**43:27** And he asked concerning their welfare [וַיִּשְׁאַל לָהֶם לְשָׁלוֹם] and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Is your aged father well [הֲשָׁלוֹם אֲבִיכֶם הַזָּקֵן] of whom you spoke [אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם] — is he still alive [הַעוֹדֶנּוּ חָי]?

**43:28** And they said [וַיֹּאמְרוּ]: Your servant our father is well [שָׁלוֹם לְעַבְדְּךָ לְאָבִינוּ] — he is still alive [עוֹדֶנּוּ חָי] — and they bowed [וַיִּקְּדוּ] and prostrated themselves [וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ].

**43:29** And he lifted up his eyes [וַיִּשָּׂא עֵינָיו] and saw [וַיַּרְא] Benjamin his brother [אֶת-בִּנְיָמִין אָחִיו] — the son of his mother [בֶּן-אִמּוֹ] — and he said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Is this your youngest brother [הֲזֶה אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן] of whom you spoke to me [אֲשֶׁר אֲמַרְתֶּם אֵלָי] — and he said [וַיֹּאמַר]: God be gracious to you [יְחָנְּךָ אֱלֹהִים] my son [בְּנִי].

**43:30** And Joseph hurried [וַיְמַהֵר יוֹסֵף] — for his compassion burned [כִּי-נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו] for his brother [אֶל-אָחִיו] and he sought [וַיְבַקֵּשׁ] to weep [לִבְכּוֹת] — and he entered [וַיָּבֹא] the inner chamber [הַחַדְרָה] and wept there [וַיֵּבְךְּ שָׁמָּה].

**43:31** And he washed his face [וַיִּרְחַץ פָּנָיו] and came out [וַיֵּצֵא] and controlled himself [וַיִּתְאַפַּק] and said [וַיֹּאמֶר]: Set on bread [שִׂימוּ לָחֶם].

---

*The Meal — Egyptians Cannot Eat with Hebrews — Benjamin's Five Portions (vv. 32–34)*

**43:32** And they set [וַיָּשִׂימוּ] for him by himself [לוֹ לְבַדּוֹ] and for them by themselves [וְלָהֶם לְבַדָּם] and for the Egyptians eating with him [וְלַמִּצְרִים הָאֹכְלִים אִתּוֹ] by themselves [לְבַדָּם] — for the Egyptians are not able [כִּי לֹא יוּכְלוּן הַמִּצְרִים] to eat bread [לֶאֱכֹל אֶת-הָעִבְרִים] with the Hebrews [לֶחֶם] — for it is an abomination [כִּי-תוֹעֵבָה הִוא] to Egypt [לְמִצְרָיִם].

**43:33** And they sat [וַיֵּשְׁבוּ] before him [לְפָנָיו] — the firstborn [הַבְּכֹר] according to his birthright [כִּבְכֹרָתוֹ] and the youngest [וְהַצָּעִיר] according to his youth [כִּצְעִירָתוֹ] — and the men marveled [וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים] each to his fellow [אִישׁ אֶל-רֵעֵהוּ].

**43:34** And he lifted [וַיִּשָּׂא] portions [מַשְׂאֹת] to them from before him [מֵאֵת פָּנָיו] — and Benjamin's portion [וַתֵּרֶב מַשְׂאַת בִּנְיָמִן] was multiplied [וַתֵּרֶב] five times [חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת] over all their portions [מִמַּשְׂאֹת כֻּלָּם] — and they drank [וַיִּשְׁתּוּ] and were drunk [וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ] with him [עִמּוֹ].

---

## Philological Notes

### 43:1 — וְהָרָעָב כָּבֵד בָּאָרֶץ (veha-ra'av kaved ba'arets)
"And the famine was heavy in the land." The chapter opens with a single-clause summary: the pressure of famine is the engine driving all that follows. The adjective כָּבֵד (kaved, "heavy") is the same root as כָּבוֹד (kavod, "glory, weight, honor"). The physical weight of famine — pressing on the household until all the grain is gone — is what finally breaks Jacob's resistance. The compressed syntax (no verb of being needed — the adjective predicates directly) mimics the directness of the pressure itself. Cf. 41:31: "the famine will be very heavy [כָּבֵד מְאֹד]" — Joseph's prediction now at full force.

### 43:3 — הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ הָאִישׁ (ha'ed he'id vanu ha'ish)
"The man solemnly warned us." The verb עוּד (ud) in the hiphil with the infinitive absolute (הָעֵד הֵעִד) is one of the strongest Hebrew emphatic constructions — "he emphatically and solemnly warned, he really warned us." The infinitive absolute before a finite verb intensifies the certainty and weight of the action. Judah is telling his father not merely that Joseph gave instructions but that he gave a solemn, witnessed, formal warning. The particle בָּנוּ ("against us" / "to us") adds a sense of the warning being lodged upon them. LXX renders μαρτυρόμενος ἐμαρτύρατο ἡμῖν ("bearing witness he testified to us"), preserving the emphatic double construction.

### 43:9 — אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ (anokhi e'ervennu)
"I myself will be surety for him." This is the theological and narrative hinge of the chapter. The verb עָרַב (arav, "to pledge, to be surety for") means to stand in for another, to take personal liability. But it is the personal pronoun אָנֹכִי (anokhi, "I myself") — the emphatic first person — that carries the weight. Judah does not say "we" or "one of us" — he says anokhi, the full, unqualified "I." This is the beginning of Judah's transformation from the man who proposed selling Joseph (37:26-27) to the man who offers himself as slave in Benjamin's place (44:33). The speech is a promise with teeth: "from my hand you may seek him" — the language of blood-accountability. Reuben's earlier offer (42:37) had been a reckless proposal to kill his own sons; Judah's is a measured, personal oath. The LXX renders ἐγὼ παρακατατίθεμαι αὐτόν ("I myself deposit/pledge him as security"), using the commercial language of a formal surety transaction.

### 43:9 — וְחָטָאתִי לְךָ כָּל-הַיָּמִים (vechatati lecha kol-hayyamim)
"Then I will have sinned against you all the days." The phrase "all the days" (כָּל-הַיָּמִים) means "for all time, forever." Judah is pledging not just his future but his moral standing permanently. The word חָטָא (chata', "to sin, to miss the mark") is the standard word for sin against a person as well as against God. Judah is putting his permanent moral identity on the line. The irony is profound: the man who once failed to protect a brother (Joseph) now pledges his permanent moral standing on protecting another.

### 43:11 — מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ (mizzimrat ha'arets)
"From the choicest produce of the land." The word זִמְרָה (zimrah) here is puzzling — it appears in only one other place (Ps 81:3 and Isa 25:5, in those cases meaning "song, music"). Some interpreters read it as "choice fruits" or "best produce" from a sense of "that which is sung about / praised." The root זמר (zamar) can mean both "to sing/praise" and "to prune (vines)." The phrase מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ then means either "the praised/choicest of the land" or "the pruned-out produce." The context — balm, honey, spices, nuts — confirms this is the finest agricultural products of Canaan. LXX renders ἀπὸ τῶν καρπῶν τῆς γῆς ("from the fruits of the land"), choosing a general term that avoids the lexical ambiguity.

### 43:11 — The Gift-List: צֳרִי, דְּבַשׁ, נְכֹאת, לֹט, בָּטְנִים, שְׁקֵדִים
"Balm, honey, tragacanth, laudanum, pistachio nuts, almonds." This list is nearly identical to the cargo of the Ishmaelite caravan that carried Joseph down to Egypt (37:25: צֳרִי, צֳרִי וּלְכֹאת וָלֹט — balm, mastic, and laudanum). The narrator's linkage is intentional and painful: the same luxury trade goods that accompanied Joseph on his descent into slavery now accompany his brothers on their descent to him. Jacob sends these gifts from Canaan to Egypt as if to an unknown powerful lord — but the goods, imbued with the memory of chapter 37, have a deeper resonance for the reader. Balm (צֳרִי, tsori) — an aromatic resin from Gilead; honey (דְּבַשׁ, devash) — either bee honey or date syrup; tragacanth (נְכֹאת, nekho't) — gum from a thorny shrub; laudanum (לֹט, lot) — labdanum, a dark fragrant resin from the rockrose plant; pistachios (בָּטְנִים, batnim) — the pistachio nut; almonds (שְׁקֵדִים, shqedim) — the beloved almond of Canaan.

### 43:14 — כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי (ka'asher shakolti shakalti)
"As I am bereaved, I am bereaved." This is one of the most haunting phrases in all of Genesis. Jacob's words are neither prayer nor lament exactly — they are a kind of resignation that has crossed over into surrender. The verb שָׁכַל (shakal, "to be bereaved of children") appears twice — the first as a perfect of existing state ("as indeed I am already bereaved"), the second as a declarative perfect ("I am bereaved"). Many translations render "if I am to be bereaved, I will be bereaved" — reading a conditional — but the Hebrew grammar does not require this; it may simply be a resigned acceptance of the truth. Compare Ruth 1:21 (Naomi's "I went away full and the LORD brought me back empty") for a similar tonality of ruined-completeness. Jacob does not say "God will protect them" — he says "I am bereaved." El Shaddai is invoked in the same breath (v.14a), but Jacob's faith is thin here — it is faith at the end of its tether.

### 43:14 — וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יִתֵּן לָכֶם רַחֲמִים (ve'El Shaddai yitten lakhem rachamim)
"And El Shaddai — may he give you mercy." El Shaddai is preserved untranslated per established convention. This is the only invocation of El Shaddai in the Joseph narrative; it occurs at the moment when Jacob releases control — he cannot compel the outcome, so he appeals to the divine name that has accompanied Israel's patriarchal journeys (17:1; 28:3; 35:11; 48:3). The word רַחֲמִים (rachamim, "mercy, compassion, womb-love") is the plural of רֶחֶם (rechem, "womb") — indicating the deep, visceral, maternal quality of divine mercy. Jacob prays that the hidden Egyptian lord — who is of course his own son — will feel compassion. The irony is multiple: the one before whom Jacob prays for mercy is Joseph himself, who is already weeping at the sight of Benjamin (v.30).

### 43:23 — נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן בְּאַמְתְּחֹתֵיכֶם (natan lakhem matmon be'amtechotekhem)
"Has given you treasure in your sacks." The steward's response is one of the most theologically striking moments in the chapter. He says neither "the silver must have been a mistake" nor "Joseph returned it deliberately." Instead, he declares: "your God and the God of your father has given you treasure." The word מַטְמוֹן (matmon, "treasure, hidden thing") comes from טָמַן (taman, "to hide, to bury"). What was hidden — money placed back in the sacks by Joseph's secret command — the steward attributes to divine action. This is also the only place in the Joseph story where an Egyptian official refers to Israel's God — and he does so with the full patriarchal formula "your God and the God of your father." Whether the steward is a crypto-believer, or whether Joseph has coached him, the text leaves open.

### 43:29 — יְחָנְּךָ אֱלֹהִים בְּנִי (yechonnekha Elohim beni)
"God be gracious to you, my son." Joseph calls Benjamin "my son" (בְּנִי) — a form of address used by an elder to a younger person or an expression of affectionate concern, not necessarily biological parenthood. The verb חָנַן (chanan, "to be gracious") in the jussive third person: "may God be gracious to you." Joseph cannot give Benjamin his blessing directly — he is still in disguise. So he channels the blessing through God's name. The Hebrew audience would note the echo of the priestly blessing: יְחָנְּךָ (yechonnekha) — the same root and form used in Num 6:25 "may the LORD be gracious to you." This is the first time Joseph has spoken directly to Benjamin.

### 43:30 — כִּי-נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו (ki-nikmeru rachamav)
"For his compassion burned / his womb-love was stirred." The verb כָּמַר (kamar) in the niphal means "to grow warm, to be stirred deeply, to burn with feeling." It appears in only two other places: 1 Kings 3:26 where the true mother's compassion is stirred for her child before Solomon, and Hos 11:8 where God's compassion "burns" for Ephraim. In all three cases the verb describes an overwhelming, involuntary surge of love. The noun רַחֲמִים (rachamim) here paired with the verb creates an image of love so intense it becomes physically unbearable — Joseph cannot stay in the room. The same pairing of womb-love and bodily overflow will occur in 45:1 when Joseph finally breaks down before all his brothers. Here in chapter 43 it is private; the weeping is in a hidden room. The public weeping must wait.

### 43:32 — תּוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרָיִם (to'evah hi' lemitsrayim)
"For it is an abomination to Egypt." The word תּוֹעֵבָה (to'evah, "abomination, something detestable") is a strong term — the same word used throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy for practices that are deeply repugnant before God. Here it is Egypt that has the to'evah: eating with Hebrews. The narrative presents this without moral commentary — this is simply how things are in Egypt. The seating arrangement at the meal — Joseph alone, the brothers alone, the Egyptians alone — reflects actual Egyptian ritual purity concerns about foreign contact during eating. The Greeks and Romans would later note similar Egyptian food-separation practices (Herodotus 2.41). The irony within the story is that Joseph himself is an Egyptian official — yet he too must eat separately from the Egyptians because he is hosting Semitic foreigners. The further irony: Joseph is a Hebrew eating alone because he cannot yet eat with his own family.

### 43:33 — וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים אִישׁ אֶל-רֵעֵהוּ (vayyitmahu ha'anashim ish el-re'ehu)
"And the men marveled each to his fellow." The verb תָּמַה (tamah, "to be astonished, to be bewildered") captures the brothers' reaction to being seated in exact birth order by someone they believe is an Egyptian stranger. They cannot explain it. The seating is not random, not coincidental — it is the exact order of their birth. They look at each other in amazement. The narrator does not tell us what they conclude — only that they are astonished. The reader knows: Joseph knows exactly who they are and the precise order of their births. He arranged the seating. The brothers' marvel is the reader's pleasure.

### 43:34 — חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת (chamesh yadot)
"Five hands/portions." The word יָד (yad) literally means "hand" but idiomatically means "a measure, portion" — "five hands of portions" = five times as much. This is a concrete expression: the "hand" is the measure used for serving, and five hands means five servings. Benjamin receives five times what any of his brothers receives. Joseph cannot yet reveal himself, but he can show favoritism — and the favoritism to Benjamin (the other son of Rachel, Joseph's own full brother) is both an expression of love and a test: will the brothers resent Benjamin's favored treatment as they once resented Joseph's favored coat? The narrative subtly recalls the original crime. LXX renders ἡ δὲ μερὶς Βενιαμιν ἐπλεόναζεν ἑπταπλασίως — "the portion of Benjamin exceeded by sevenfold" — reading שֶׁבַע (seven) instead of חָמֵשׁ (five), a textual variant.

### 43:34 — וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ (vayyishtu vayyishkeru immo)
"And they drank and were drunk with him." The verb שָׁכַר (shakar, "to be drunk, to drink freely/abundantly") does not necessarily indicate incapacitating intoxication — in some contexts it means "to drink deeply, to drink freely, to have one's fill of drink." The Septuagint renders ἐμεθύσθησαν ("they were made drunk"), choosing the stronger reading. The scene is remarkable: after years of separation, violence, guilt, and tension — Joseph and his brothers eat and drink together, and the drinking is abundant. The emotional weight of the reunion — still incomplete, still concealed — finds expression in a shared feast. There is something poignant here: before the truth can come out, before the tears of full recognition in chapter 45, there is this — an unknowing family reunion around a table, full of food and drink, the brothers not yet knowing who their host is.

---

## LXX Comparison Table (Vaticanus, pp. 43–44)

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus Greek) | Significant Divergence |
|-------|-------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 43:3 | הָעֵד הֵעִד בָּנוּ הָאִישׁ — "the man solemnly warned us" | μαρτυρόμενος ἐμαρτύρατο ἡμῖν ὁ ἄνθρωπος — "bearing witness the man testified to us" | LXX preserves the emphatic double construction faithfully |
| 43:7 | שָׁאוֹל שָׁאַל הָאִישׁ — "the man asked insistently" | ἐρωτῶν ἐπηρώτησεν ἡμᾶς ὁ ἄνθρωπος — "questioning he questioned us" | LXX preserves the emphatic double construction with ἐρωτάω |
| 43:9 | אָנֹכִי אֶעֶרְבֶנּוּ — "I myself will be surety for him" | ἐγὼ παρακατατίθεμαι αὐτόν — "I myself deposit him as pledge" | LXX uses commercial pledge language (παρακατατίθεμαι); MT's אָרַב is equally formal surety |
| 43:11 | מִזִּמְרַת הָאָרֶץ — "from the choicest produce of the land" | ἀπὸ τῶν καρπῶν τῆς γῆς — "from the fruits of the land" | LXX avoids the ambiguous זִמְרָה with a general term; loses the possible "praised produce" nuance |
| 43:14 | כַּאֲשֶׁר שָׁכֹלְתִּי שָׁכָלְתִּי — "as I am bereaved, I am bereaved" | εἰ δὲ τέκνων ἠτέκνωμαι, ἠτέκνωμαι — "but if I am bereaved of children, I am bereaved" | LXX adds the conditional particle εἰ (if), making the resignation more explicitly conditional; MT may be simply resigned statement |
| 43:16 | וּטְבֹחַ טֶבַח — "and slaughter a slaughter" | καὶ σφάξον θύματα — "and slaughter sacrificial animals" | LXX uses θύματα (sacrificial victims/offerings) for MT's simple "slaughter"; slightly elevates the meal language |
| 43:23 | נָתַן לָכֶם מַטְמוֹן — "has given you treasure" | θεὸς ὑμῶν... ἔδωκεν ὑμῖν θησαυρούς — "your God gave you treasures" | LXX uses θησαυρούς (treasures, storehouses); MT's מַטְמוֹן emphasizes the hidden/buried quality |
| 43:26 | וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אָרְצָה — "they bowed down to him to the ground" | καὶ προσεκύνησαν αὐτῷ ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν — "they prostrated themselves to him on the ground" | Identical in substance; the full prostration before Joseph fulfills the eleven-star dream of 37:9 (with Jacob yet absent) |
| 43:29 | יְחָנְּךָ אֱלֹהִים בְּנִי — "God be gracious to you, my son" | ὁ θεὸς ἐλεήσαι σε, τέκνον — "may God have mercy on you, child" | LXX uses ἐλεέω ("to have mercy") for MT's חָנַן ("to be gracious"); LXX τέκνον ("child") for MT's בְּנִי ("my son") — slight tenderness difference |
| 43:30 | כִּי-נִכְמְרוּ רַחֲמָיו — "for his compassion burned" | συνεκεχύνθη γὰρ ἡ καρδία αὐτοῦ — "for his heart was moved/confounded" | LXX uses συγχέω ("to be poured together, overwhelmed"); MT uses the rare נִכְמְרוּ with רַחֲמִים ("womb-love"); LXX generalizes to "heart" |
| 43:32 | כִּי-תוֹעֵבָה הִוא לְמִצְרָיִם — "for it is an abomination to Egypt" | βδέλυγμα γάρ ἐστιν τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις — "for it is an abomination to the Egyptians" | LXX uses βδέλυγμα, the standard LXX rendering of תּוֹעֵבָה throughout the Pentateuch; exact equivalence |
| 43:33 | וַיִּתְמְהוּ הָאֲנָשִׁים — "and the men marveled" | ἐξέστησαν δὲ οἱ ἄνθρωποι — "and the men were astonished" | LXX uses ἐξίστημι ("to be beside oneself, astonished"); MT's תָּמַה is simpler "to be amazed" |
| 43:34 | חָמֵשׁ יָדוֹת — "five portions/hands" | ἐπλεόναζεν ἡ μερὶς Βενιαμιν ἑπταπλασίως — "the portion of Benjamin exceeded sevenfold" | LXX reads שֶׁבַע (seven) against MT's חָמֵשׁ (five) — significant textual variant; MT "five" is well attested in all Hebrew MSS |
| 43:34 | וַיִּשְׁתּוּ וַיִּשְׁכְּרוּ עִמּוֹ — "they drank and were drunk with him" | ἔπιον δὲ καὶ ἐμεθύσθησαν μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ — "they drank and were made drunk with him" | LXX uses μεθύσκω (to make drunk); MT's שָׁכַר can mean abundant/free drinking without full intoxication |

---

## Structural Note

Genesis 43 is the second movement of the Joseph resolution arc — the chapter of tested hesitation, delayed reunion, and concealed recognition. Where chapter 42 ended in paralysis (Jacob refusing to send Benjamin, the household grinding to a halt), chapter 43 is the chapter of motion — forced by famine, authorized by Judah's surety, and punctuated by Joseph's barely-contained emotion.

**Architectural shape:** The chapter follows a chiastic-like descent-and-ascent pattern. It opens with weight (כָּבֵד, the heavy famine) and closes with abundance (Benjamin's five portions, the communal drinking). Between these poles, three negotiations take place: the family negotiation at home (vv.1-14), the negotiation at Joseph's door (vv.18-23), and the negotiation-free feast (vv.32-34). Each negotiation ends with release of anxiety: Jacob relents and releases Benjamin; the steward releases the brothers from fear and releases Simeon; the feast releases months of tension into laughter and wine.

**Judah's emergence (vv.8-10):** Genesis 43 is the first chapter in which Judah becomes the operative spokesman of the brothers, replacing Reuben. The shift is theologically significant. Reuben's surety offer in 42:37 ("kill my two sons") was emotionally reckless and practically worthless; Jacob had rejected it. Judah's offer in 43:9 is different in kind: "I myself will be surety" — a formal, personal pledge with moral permanence. The Judah of chapter 38, who failed Tamar, and the Judah of chapter 37, who proposed selling Joseph, is being transformed. This verse is the beginning of that transformation, which will reach its climax in 44:33 with Judah's offer to become a slave in Benjamin's place — the most complete act of self-substitution in Genesis.

**The gift-list and memory (v.11):** Jacob's list of gifts — balm, honey, tragacanth, laudanum, pistachios, almonds — is a near-repetition of the Ishmaelite caravan's cargo in 37:25 (balm, mastic, laudanum). The narrator is drawing a line from the commerce in Joseph's body to the commerce that now seeks to buy favor before him. Jacob does not know this. The reader does. The gifts that once accompanied Joseph as a piece of cargo now accompany his brothers as a peace offering to the man they sold.

**El Shaddai invoked (v.14):** Jacob's invocation of El Shaddai — the patriarchal name used at moments of radical dependence on divine action (17:1; 28:3; 35:11) — marks the theological register of the moment. El Shaddai is the name associated with fruitfulness, multiplication, and divine sufficiency. Jacob cannot guarantee Benjamin's return; no human negotiation can. He releases control and names the God who governs outcomes his human planning cannot reach. But the invocation is immediately followed by the darkest line in the chapter: "As I am bereaved, I am bereaved." The faith and the grief are simultaneous.

**Three seatings (v.32):** The seating arrangement — Joseph alone, the brothers alone, the Egyptians alone — is a threefold separation that maps the narrative's concealment perfectly. Joseph cannot sit with his brothers because the Egyptians would find it to'evah; the Egyptians cannot sit with the brothers for the same reason. The result is that Joseph is isolated even from the Egyptians at his own table — surrounded by people he cannot yet be with. The separation enforced by Egyptian ritual purity becomes an accidental symbol of the deeper separation — a man eating near his brothers but not yet known to them.

**Benjamin's portions and the coat (v.34):** The five-fold portion given to Benjamin is Joseph's first covert act of love directed at his full brother. It recalls and reverses the כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים (the coat of many colors/long sleeves, 37:3) — the visible favoritism that destroyed the family. There Jacob's visible love for Joseph had stirred the brothers' hatred. Here Joseph tests whether the brothers will react to visible favoritism toward Benjamin with the same resentment. The test is implicit, not yet explicit. Chapter 44 will make it explicit. The brothers' reaction here — they drink freely, they are merry — suggests they do not resent Benjamin's portion. The transformation Judah has begun in verse 9 may have spread further than Joseph knows.

**The private weeping (v.30):** Joseph's flight to the inner chamber is the emotional center of the chapter. Twice in the Joseph story he has wept before his brothers: here (private, concealed, in a hidden room) and in 45:2 (public, before all of them, his voice heard by Pharaoh's house). The distance between those two weepings is the distance of one chapter and one final test. In 43:30, Joseph cannot yet afford to weep where anyone can see him — he is not yet ready to reveal himself, and to reveal himself he must first be sure his brothers have changed. The womb-love (רַחֲמִים, rachamim) that burns for Benjamin in verse 30 is the same word Jacob uses when he invokes El Shaddai to grant mercy (v.14). Father and son, in different places, in different registers — both reach toward the same deep, womb-rooted compassion. The narrative is quietly completing a circle.
